Regulatory decisions in one major market have sometimes influenced how companies operate globally.

category_context.why_it_matters: Climate and weather events increasingly intersect with agriculture, insurance, energy demand, and infrastructure planning.
typical_channels: Crop yields and food prices can be affected by extreme weather in major growing regions.
Insurance and reinsurance costs in an affected region have historically risen after severe events.
Energy demand for heating or cooling, and infrastructure resilience spending, can shift with climate trends.

headlines.title: Sudan inflation jumps to 51.28% in June as currency plunges - Sudan Tribune

ripple_effect.name: Sharp currency devaluation / currency crisis

historical_precedents.what_happened: Thailand's decision to float its currency, the baht, after months of speculative pressure triggered a wave of currency devaluations across Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, and other Asian economies.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Several regional currencies lost half or more of their value against the U.S. dollar within months, stock markets across the region fell sharply, and multiple countries required IMF bailout programs.

historical_precedents.what_happened: The Turkish lira came under severe pressure amid high inflation, large foreign-currency debts, and a diplomatic dispute with the U.S.
historical_precedents.market_impact: The lira lost roughly 30–40% of its value against the U.S. dollar over the year, sharply raising the cost of imports and foreign-currency debt for Turkish businesses.

possibility_chains: A sharp currency devaluation has historically made imported goods, including fuel and food, significantly more expensive for consumers and businesses in the affected country.

Companies and governments with large debts denominated in a foreign currency (commonly the U.S. dollar) have historically faced sharply higher effective debt burdens when their local currency devalues.

A currency crisis in one emerging-market economy has historically raised investor caution toward other economies seen as having similar vulnerabilities, sometimes spreading pressure regionally even without a direct economic link.

headlines.title: Oil prices jump 3% as US-Iran conflict intensifies, Brent crude tops $90 per barrel

ripple_effect.name: Persian Gulf oil supply shock

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iran and Iraq attacked oil tankers transiting the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz, and Iran repeatedly threatened to close the strait.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Shipping insurance war-risk premiums in the Gulf rose sharply, and the U.S. Navy began escorting reflagged Kuwaiti tankers in 1987 to keep the strait open.

historical_precedents.event: Drone and missile attack on Saudi Aramco's Abqaiq and Khurais facilities
historical_precedents.what_happened: The attack (which Saudi Arabia and the U.S. blamed on Iran, a claim Iran denied) temporarily knocked out roughly half of Saudi Arabia's oil output, about 5% of global supply.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Brent crude jumped about 15% in a single day — the largest one-day oil price increase since the 1990–91 Gulf War.

historical_precedents.what_happened: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed a large share of Gulf oil exports from the market and raised fears of a wider regional war.
historical_precedents.market_impact: Oil prices roughly doubled in the months following the invasion before falling back once the war ended and supply concerns eased.

possibility_chains: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil is estimated to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, so a sustained blockage or major disruption there has historically driven crude oil prices sharply higher.

Because ammonia-based fertilizer production is natural-gas intensive, and natural gas prices often move together with oil, a prolonged energy price spike has historically fed through into higher fertilizer costs over the following months.
Fertilizer is a major input cost in food production, so energy shocks of this kind have historically been followed — often with a lag — by higher food prices.
Shipping insurers have historically raised war-risk premiums for vessels transiting the Gulf during periods of heightened tension, adding to the landed cost of oil and other cargo.
